title: Saddle stitching leather by hand
subtitle: Two needles, one thread, each stitch locked
excerpt: How to saddle stitch leather using the two-needle method: stitching chiselling the holes, threading the needles on one length of waxed thread, and making each stitch with both needles so the thread is locked at every point.
sourceNotes: Traditional European leatherwork practice. Al Stohlman The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (1977). City & Guilds Leatherwork Level 2. Two-needle technique unchanged from medieval guild practice.

content.text: is stronger than a sewing-machine stitch because each pass of the thread is locked in place by the second needle. If one thread breaks, the stitch does not unravel; it holds at the break point. For this reason, it is the standard for all structural leather seams where the join must hold under sustained load.
Linen or polyester at 0.6 to 1.0 mm. Waxed thread slides through the holes easily and the wax binds the knot on finishing. Match the thread colour to the leather or contrast deliberately.
Blunt-tipped curved or straight needles with an eye large enough for the thread. Blunt needles slide through pre-pricked holes without piercing the leather edge.
A multi-prong tool that creates evenly-spaced holes. 3.5 to 4.5 mm pitch is standard for most leatherwork.
A floor-standing or bench clamp that holds the work at eye level, leaving both hands free for the needles.

Scribe a stitch line parallel to the edge of the leather at 3 to 4 mm from the edge using a stitching groover or overstitch wheel. This line marks the hole positions and recesses the thread so it sits below the leather surface and is protected from abrasion.

Hold the leather flat on a hard surface (a marble or granite offcut is ideal). Position the stitching chisel on the marked line and drive it through the leather thickness with the mallet. Repeat along the full stitch line, positioning the last prong of the previous strike in the last hole of the previous mark to maintain even spacing.

Cut a length of thread about four times the stitch length. Thread one needle at each end of the same length of thread. Lock each needle by piercing the thread through itself: pass the needle through the thread at about 25 mm from the end, pull the short tail back through the needle eye, and pull firm. This locks the needle on the thread so it does not slip off during stitching.

Push needle 1 through the first hole from front to back. Pull through, leaving the two thread ends approximately equal in length. Push needle 2 through the same hole from back to front. Both needles are now through the first hole, one on each side of the leather.

Push needle 1 through the second hole front to back. Before pulling it through, pass needle 2 through the same hole back to front, but pass it in front of (behind, in the stitch direction) the thread carried by needle 1. Pull both needles through with even tension, drawing the stitch snug. This locking pass is the defining feature of the saddle stitch. Repeat for each hole.

At the last hole, reverse direction and stitch back through the previous 2 to 3 holes to lock the thread. Tie a single half-hitch from one thread to the other. Trim the thread ends as close as possible to the leather surface with a sharp knife. Apply a touch of heat from a lighter briefly to melt and seal the polyester thread ends, or burnish linen ends into the groove.
Thread not locking and forming a loop on one side.

Needle 2 is not being passed in front of needle 1's thread before pulling through. The crossing of the threads inside the hole is what makes the lock; if both needles pass in the same plane, the stitch is a simple running stitch, not a saddle stitch.
The stitching chisel was not repositioned with the last prong in the previous hole. Consistent spacing requires this overlap at every step; without it the holes drift.
